text: In this video we aim to introduce two surgical approaches for duodenal Crohn's disease management, the gastrojejunal bypass and the duodenal jejunal bypass. Duodenal Crohn's disease is considered a rare entity. Most of these patients present with a structuring Crohn's disease phenotype and usually with concurrent involvement of other several locations of the gastrointestinal tract. Similar to other locations, duodenal Crohn's disease management includes several pharmacological options. Surgery is considered in patients with refractory duodenal Crohn's disease. Surgical options include bypass approaches and duodenal strictureplasty. The following slide summarizes the duodenal Crohn's disease pattern of involvement. Duodenal first portion is the most commonly affected portion. It usually implies a continuous involvement of an antropolar disease. As we can appreciate in the radiological variegated transit, second and third portions involvement usually causes a single structure. Finally, Crohn's disease affecting the fourth portion frequently causes long or several strictures, with potential involvement of contiguous proximal jejunum.

We present a clinical case of a sixty-seven year old patient diagnosed of duodenal Crohn's disease affecting the duodenal fourth portion. The patient presented with repetitive vomiting and oral intolerance. Complementary tests, including upper GI endoscopy and enteric MRI, showed an estrictory pattern with long duodenal involvement. Conservative medical therapy, including azatropine and infliximab, failed. Endoscopy dilatation was also attained, with no significant improvement. Therefore, surgical treatment was indicated. As we can see in the video, the patient has a Crohn's disease with a stricture in the involvement of duodenal fourth portion. Considering the potential complexity of duodenal resection or strictureplasty, a minimally invasive gastrointestinal bypass was decided to be the best alternative. We mobilized the greater omentum in order to get access to the lesser sac and posterior gastric wall. A side-to-side mechanical gastrojejunal anastomosis is performed. The remaining enterotomy is closed using a running long-term absorbable suture. Two weeks after the patient's discharge from the hospital, he returned to the emergency room reporting similar symptoms. A fluoroscopic double-contrast EGD study along with an upper GI endoscopy were performed, revealing a permeable gastrojejunal anastomosis. However, the presence of a severe esophagitis due to bioreflux in the context of a severe duodenal stenosis was present. Additional surgery was indicated. In this case, as is displayed in the surgical drawing, a minimally invasive duodenal jejunal bypass was performed. The patient was discharged with complete symptomatic relief.

The surgeon identifies the Treitz ligament and explores the abdominal cavity, revealing typical L-four involvement affecting the jejunum with creeping fat and intestinal serosa congestion. A duodenal jejunal anastomosis is performed using a side-to-side mechanical technique, followed by closure of the enterotomy with a long-term absorbable continuous running suture.

In conclusion, although gastrojejunal bypass is the most frequently performed surgical procedure in duodenal Crohn's disease, this should be only considered in patients with stenosis above the second duodenal portion. In obstructive strictures, distal to the second duodenal portion, duodenal jejunal bypass should be considered. This will minimize the potentially associated symptomatic pyroflux.
